findings.description: Header layout differs: PDF has a centered two-line department title with address info below, while HTML separates department name and division name with a horizontal rule between them. Officials' names are laid out differently (side-by-side in PDF vs. stacked in HTML).
findings.evidence: PDF: 'North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services / Division of Social Services' as one centered block with Governor/Director names flanking left and right. HTML: Department name centered with horizontal rule, then Division name, then address, then officials listed vertically on the left.

findings.description: The 'Re:' subject line formatting differs slightly. In the PDF it appears as bold right-aligned text, while in HTML it appears as bold left-aligned underlined text.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Re: 2002 DOR and TOP Collection Fees / 2002 DOR Interest Rate' right-aligned, bold, no underline. HTML: Same text left-aligned, bold, underlined.

findings.description: The reference code 'EI-5-2002' formatting is preserved as bold in both versions, consistent.

The HTML uses both h1 and h2 for the department header, but the document title and division name are both part of the letterhead, not a true heading hierarchy. More importantly, the h2 'Division of Social Services' appears under h1 which is acceptable, but the nested <main> elements create structural issues.
findings.evidence: HTML has <h1> for 'North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services' and <h2> for 'Division of Social Services'. These are letterhead elements in the PDF, not document sections.

findings.description: The HTML contains nested <main> elements - there is an outer <main> from the body template and an inner <main> within the content, which is an HTML structural error but does not duplicate visible content.
findings.evidence: Outer: <body><main> wrapping everything. Inner: another <main> tag after the page-header div.
